words: This conversation with Jean Wilson Ringsage and Stiner Ringsage took place at
their home in Park on October 19, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.

speaker: JWR
words: She had no idea what the prairie was like at that.moment. She wasn't

speaker: JWR
words: living on it. She was living in Vancouver, B.C. She was fortunate

speaker: JWR
words: enough to still have a job managing another hotel. And she had not

speaker: JWR
words: idea what it was like on the prairie.

speaker: SS
words: About how rough it was?

speaker: JWR
words: No idea. I never told her. use worrying her about it.

speaker: SS
words: Really?

speaker: JWR
words: Huh-uh.

speaker: SS
words: You mean, you didn't advise her just how rough it was?

speaker: JWR
words: Not til after the fact :, no. No, you don't. No, we weren't dying,

speaker: JWR
words: we weren't starving to death, exactly. We certainly had an unbalan

speaker: JWR
words: ced diet. It wasn't I didn't confide in her, but there's no use—

speaker: JWR
words: nice things and, oh, some of the rough things, when theirs apart.

words: Not too rough.

words: you have to.

speaker: SS

speaker: JWR

speaker: JWR
words: I was going to get away from Mother's apron strings when I married

speaker: JWR
words: Stiner. I married the eternal optimist, like my mother. She was al

speaker: JWR
words: ways looking ahead- things were- you know the Unsinkable Molly

speaker: JWR
words: Brown- you know that spirit, nothing ever sunk her, nothing ever

speaker: JWR
words: really— she'd look at the bright side: It isn't going to last and

speaker: JWR
words: she always seemed to manage. Things meshed in so that she came out

speaker: JWR
words: and she was on top. He isn't quite so good as she was. I don't know

speaker: JWR
words: I don't know, I'm not too sure about him but she was a pretty devout

speaker: JWR
words: woman in her way. She was unorthodox but she was a true believer.

speaker: SS
words: So you call it the decade, that you grew up in?

speaker: JWR
words: It was. She was. That beautiful. The days— well, it was in the

speaker: JWR
words: 1890's, you know. It was very genteel. People were polite and

speaker: JWR
words: courteous, and you went to visit a lady and Mother left calling cards,

speaker: JWR
words: you know, it's the correct thing. And, I don't know, you didn't in

speaker: JWR
words: trude, and yet you were there with sympathy and help. And you had

speaker: JWR
words: high ideals. And, as I say, even the town drunk had his principles.

speaker: JWR
words: You know and the ladies, well they had their principles, too. They

speaker: JWR
words: were just like gunsmoke and they had their hearts of gold amongst

words: those. They were around. They were beautiful. And we'd pass 'em

words: in the street, and five years old and ten or eight— mmmmm - they

words: didn't fill in the details, but you realized they lived a life apart

speaker: JWR
words: from yours. But I don't think the bubble started to break til after

words: World War I, and then it went down fast. But it was Horatio Alger,

speaker: JWR
words: you were always going to— the good guy won out. Lady was virtuous

speaker: JWR
words: and so on and she grew up to a ripe old age and her children rose up

speaker: JWR
words: and called her- you know, gave her honor and so on.

speaker: SS

speaker: JWR

speaker: JWR

speaker: JWR
words: ones that are lingering in hospitals now; yes, war was terrible. But

speaker: JWR
words: oh, the years of hunger. The years of just living on the edge of

speaker: JWR
words: nothing. Look, it started with us in '28 went through to '34-

speaker: JWR
words: what's that? Six years. And, you weren't dying, but you were just

words: a year, but they didn't know when I appliedAI was married. I hadn't

speaker: SS
words: They stayed at

speaker: JWR

speaker: JWR

speaker: JWR

speaker: JWR
